coredata.dc:title: Aphen-derived N-doped white-emitting carbon dots with room temperature phosphorescence for versatile applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Feifei
Zhang, Tianyi
Liu, Qian
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: For most of the carbon dots (CDs), solid-state fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) are difficult to achieve due to self-quenching and intersystem crossing (ISC) limitations. Here, nitrogen-doped CDs-1 showing both fluorescence and RTP was obtained by hydrothermal reaction from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen). The CDs-1 emits blue-green fluorescence in aqueous solution, while emits white fluorescence and yellow-green RTP in the solid state with a RTP lifetime up to 103 ms, exhibiting a long afterglow with a duration of 3 s, which is visible to the naked eyes. The RTP of the CDs-1 is derived from the CN/CN in Aphen and CN/CN in Aphen and CO produced during the carbonization process with a small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states, which helps to realize the ISC. At the same time, the hydrogen bonds between the CN/CN or CO bonds and OH of the not completely carbonized PVA chains on the CDs-1 surface have the advantages of moisture resistance, oxygen barrier and improving the rigidity of CDs-1. Eventually, non-radiative transitions can be suppressed. Based on the decent fluorescence properties, the aqueous solution of CDs-1 can be used to detect TNP and is responsive to pH with a good linear relationship between acid and alkali. In addition, the RTP of CDs-1 can be utilized as banknote anti-counterfeiting material under a long excitation wavelength. This work provides a possibility to realize the multi-mode illumination regulation of CDs by facile one-step strategy.

head.ce:title: Aphen-derived N-doped white-emitting carbon dots with room temperature phosphorescence for versatile applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work provides a facile construction method of multiple applications of room temperature phosphorescence and fluorescence CDs-1 derived from Aphen and PVA for TNP detection and pH response in aqueous solution as well as the anti-counterfeiting application to banknotes in solid state.

For most of the carbon dots (CDs), solid-state fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) are difficult to achieve due to self-quenching and intersystem crossing (ISC) limitations. Here, nitrogen-doped CDs-1 showing both fluorescence and RTP was obtained by hydrothermal reaction from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen). The CDs-1 emits blue-green fluorescence in aqueous solution, while emits white fluorescence and yellow-green RTP in the solid state with a RTP lifetime up to 103 ms, exhibiting a long afterglow with a duration of 3 s, which is visible to the naked eyes. The RTP of the CDs-1 is derived from the CN/CN in Aphen and CN/CN in Aphen and CO produced during the carbonization process with a small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states, which helps to realize the ISC. At the same time, the hydrogen bonds between the CN/CN or CO bonds and OH of the not completely carbonized PVA chains on the CDs-1 surface have the advantages of moisture resistance, oxygen barrier and improving the rigidity of CDs-1. Eventually, non-radiative transitions can be suppressed. Based on the decent fluorescence properties, the aqueous solution of CDs-1 can be used to detect TNP and is responsive to pH with a good linear relationship between acid and alkali. In addition, the RTP of CDs-1 can be utilized as banknote anti-counterfeiting material under a long excitation wavelength. This work provides a possibility to realize the multi-mode illumination regulation of CDs by facile one-step strategy.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their long life, excellent light stability and low toxicity, metal-free phosphorescent materials can be applied in various fields such as metal ion detection, information encryption and bio-imaging [1–5]. In recent years, phosphorescent materials have achieved many breakthrough developments, but it is difficult to synthesize non-toxic, stable, long-life room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials in one step [6–8]. Because the realization of RTP usually needs to consider the following two points: (1) general organic molecules are difficult to achieve intersystem crossing (ISC) from the singlet state to the triplet state, (2) the triplet state is easily quenched by oxygen, and the excited triplet electrons are easy to return to the ground state in the form of non-radiative transitions [9–12]. The above two issues indicate that the RTP of the material is more difficult to achieve than the fluorescence performance, which severely limits the development of metal-free RTP materials. Therefore, the researchers mainly achieve RTP by the following approaches: (1) Introducing special organic moieties or heavy atoms into the phosphor to promote the ISC of the singlet to triplet [13–15]; (2) forming crystalline materials, in which the vibration dissipation of the triplet excitons can be restricted, engendering RTP emission originating from the triplet state (T1) to the ground state (S0) [16]; (3) doping the phosphor into a solid matrix, such as polymer (polyvinyl alcohol, etc) or a porous structure (silica or cage polysilsesquioxane) to suppress the energy dissipation [17,18]. At the same time, the rigid structure also prevents oxygen quenching of the triplet state [6]. The introduction of heavy atoms generally increases the toxicity of materials, severely limiting their uses in many fields. When the illuminant is crystalline, due to the instability of the properties and the difficulty in processing, it has encountered great obstacles in practical applications [14–17]. Although phosphorescence can be produced by doping the phosphors into a polymer or other solid matrix, there are many disadvantages such as complicated preparation, high cost, and time consuming [14,19–21].

Carbon dots (CDs) are widely used in optoelectronic devices, biosensing, photocatalysis and other fields due to their wide range of raw materials, simple synthesis process, excellent luminescence performance and biocompatibility [22–26]. However, in the solid state, the CDs would suffer from aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) problem, leading to the difficulty on emitting light in the aggregate state, not to mention achieving RTP [27–30]. Some pioneering efforts on achieving RTP of CDs are mainly divided into two types. One is a two-step method: first to synthesize CDs, and then doping the CDs into the polymer matrix to achieve RTP. In 2016, Lin et al. reported that three kinds of CDs with different luminous colors were doped into PVA to gain RTP, but it is necessary to select a polymer that can react or strongly interact with CDs, which requires a lot of work, thus limiting the broader development of RTP CDs [6,9,27–29 31–33]. The other is inspired by the crosslink-inducing enhancement effect (CEE), using the polymer as a raw material to directly synthesize polymer carbon dots (PCDs). More simply, the Yang’s group used the polymer as a raw material to prepare CDs directly, so that the obtained PCDs show the efficient RTP properties, which gave us great inspiration [2]. In addition, Feng et al. introduced the element such as nitrogen into the organic molecules, arousing strong spin-orbit coupling, therefore ISC can be promoted and it is easier to achieve RTP [29,34,35]. 5-Amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen) has amino nitrogen and pyridinium nitrogen, which greatly increases the nitrogen content of the whole molecule. It is well known that Aphen has a core of phenanthroline, which has stronger rigidity, but it is difficult to achieve RTP by itself [36].

In this work, we envisage full use of the restriction of CDs on the molecules, the rigidity and high nitrogen content of Aphen itself, as well as the further limitation of the polymer, by one-step route to achieve RTP of CDs-1. CDs-1 with decent RTP and fluorescence features were synthesized based on PVA and Aphen utilizing a simple hydrothermal reaction. The CDs-1 exhibit the RTP quantum yield of 5.3% with a long lifetime of 103 ms, and the RTP is visible by the naked eye for several seconds. The fluorescence and RTP mechanism of CDs-1 were also further studied. We suspect that the CN/CN in Aphen moieties and CO in the CDs-1 can promote the efficiency of the ISC. Meanwhile, the formed hydrogen bonds between the Aphen moieties and uncarbonized PVA chains on the surface of CDs-1 inhibit the non-radiative transitions, and the rigidity of Aphen itself and the fixation of CDs-1 also are conductive to the generation of RTP. More notably, hydrogen bonds formed by PVA chains also act as the moisture and oxygen barrier. Moisture resistance prevents the water from damaging the structure of the CDs-1, thereby avoiding the oxygen to quench the triplet state. In addition, the aqueous solution of CDs-1 can be utilized as high selectively fluorescent probe for TNP with a detection limit of 153 nM, and it also has a good linear response to pH. At the same time, the RTP properties of solid-state CDs-1 were also applied to the encryption and decryption of information.

5-Amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen) was prepared according to the procedure of the literature [37]. H3PO4 was purchased from Beijing Reagent Factory. PVA (1750 ± 50), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Factory. 2,4-Dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), 2,6-dinitrotoluene (2,6-DNT), nitrobenzene (NB), nitromethane (NM), o-nitrotoluene (2-NT), 1,3-dinitrobenzene (1,3-DNB), phenol (phenol), and p-nitrophenol (p-NP) were purchased from Macklin.

PVA (0.6 g) was added to 15 mL of distilled water, and heated at 90 °C for 3 h to be completely dissolved, then 0.15 g of Aphen and 40 μL of H3PO4 was added respectively, until the mixture became a homogeneous orange-red solution. The mixed solution was transferred into an autoclave (25 mL), and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the obtained brown suspension was purged with acetone to remove excess PVA, and the precipitate was removed by centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 5 min). The centrifuged liquid was dialyzed against water (3500 Da) for 48 h, then the CDs-1 solid was obtained by freeze-drying of the dialysis liquid.

Under the optimal detection conditions, the CDs-1 was configured to be 0.3 mg mL−1 aqueous solution, and then 20 μL 1,3-DNB, NB, NM, Phenol, 4-NP, 2-NT, 4-DNT, 2,6-DNT, TNP, and TNT were added to the 3.0 mL CDs-1 solution respectively, and the final concentration of all explosives in the CDs-1 solutions was fixed at 6.68 μM. The corresponding emission spectra were tested excited with 320 nm, which is the best excitation of the aqueous solution of CDs-1. The unique response and selectivity of TNP were also further characterized. The concentration of the CDs-1 was fixed, so that the concentrations of TNP in the solution were 1.67, 3.34, 6.68, 13.36, 20.04, 26.72, 33.40 μM, respectively, and the detection conditions are the same as above. Finally, we combined the relevant characterizations and a series of tests to testify the detection mechanism of TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CDs-1 aqueous solution to pH
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-1 was dispersed by ultrasound in the different BR buffer solutions with a final concentration of 0.3 mg mL−1, and the pH values of various buffer solutions were determined to be 2.25, 2.81, 3.51, 4.02, 4.51, 5.03, 5.52, 6.56, 7.01, 8.19, 8.62, 9.02, 9.66, 10.03, 10.67, 11.09, and 11.56, respectively, and their fluorescence emission spectra were measured with an optimum excitation of 320 nm.
The position of "1″ on the upper side of the bill was written using the dialysis liquid of CDs-1, then dried thoroughly at room temperature.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron micrographs (SEM) were obtained from the JEM-2100 F electron microscope and scanning electron microscope (SEM) (XL-30ESEM FEG, FEI Company), respectively. The Magna 560 FTIR spectrometer and the Quantum 2000 spectrometer were used to measure the infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the samples to characterize their structures. In addition, the SHIMADZU UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer and Edinburgh FLSP920 were used to characterize the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence and time-resolved luminescence spectra of samples. The phosphorescence spectra and quantum yields of the samples were measured by Hitachi F-7000 and Hamamatsu C9920-02.

The SEM image of the solid CDs-1 is shown in Fig. 1 a. It can be seen that the powders of the CDs-1 are in the form of aggregation, and connect to each other. We suspect that the PVA chains on CDs-1, which are not fully carbonized, can connect the CDs-1 to each other by hydrogen bonding [21,32]. In order to prove the above conclusion, we dispersed the solid CDs-1 in ethanol to observe their TEM morphology. As deposited in Fig. 1b, there are many flakes connected to each other. It can be seen that the aggregation pattern of CDs-1 in ethanol is mostly retained, and the CDs-1 is not completely separated [32]. However, when the solid CDs-1 was ultrasonically dispersed in water, the CDs-1 particles with an average size of 11.3 nm can be observed in Fig. 1c and d. In addition, as displayed in HRTEM (inset of Fig. 1c) of CDs-1, the lattice of the obtained CDs-1 is not obvious, indicating that the carbonization is incomplete in the synthesis procedure of CDs-1 [38–40].

Fig. S1 is the FTIR spectra of CDs-0.5, CDs-1 and CDs-2 in dried state. It can be seen that the three types of CDs contain a large amount of OH owing to the strong absorption band at 3420 cm−1, and we suspect that it should be due to incomplete carbonization of PVA [41,42]. The incomplete carbonized PVA provides the possibility of forming hydrogen bonds, which can enhance the rigidity of the entire system, limiting the non-radiative transition, and finally achieving the RTP property of the CDs [32]. The characteristic absorption band at 1669 cm-1 is attributed to the typical stretching vibration of CO [2]. The absorption peaks at 3244, 1105 and 855–805 cm-1 are assigned to N-H, CO, and benzene derived from Aphen moieties on CDs [15]. In addition, although CDs-1 and CDs-0.5 have similar peaks, there is a certain difference between them and CDs-2 in 950–600 cm−1. Due to the different reactant ratio of Aphen, the structure formed in the carbonization process may be different, so that the benzene ring in Aphen has different restrictions, meanwhile the vibration mode of the benzene ring is also different [36,43,44].

We also utilized XPS to further analyze the element composition of CDs-1. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CDs-1 consists of three elements: C, O, and N. The corresponding binding energies at 284.3, 397.5, and 531.4 eV are ascribed to C1 s, N1 s, and O1 s, respectively. The C1 s spectrum (Fig. 2b) can be deconvolved to C–N/CO (286.3 eV) and CC/CC
(284.5 eV). The N1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c) can be divided in four peaks, which are graphitic N (402.1 eV), pyrrolic N (400.5 eV), amino N (399.2 eV), and pyridinic N (398.6 eV). The presence of the different N-containing groups indicates that Aphen has undergone a certain reaction and carbonization during the generation of the CDs-1, meanwhile some amino groups are also present in the CDs-1, besides the amide bonds. The XPS of O1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d) contains three components for CO (531.1 eV), OH bond in C(O)OH (532.3 eV), and CO
(534.2 eV) [22,27]. The above data together demonstrated the successful synthesis of CDs-1 from PVA and Aphen [43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and fluorescence properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs-1 solid is shown in Fig. S2. As depicted in Fig. S2, the strong absorption band of CDs-1 at 236 nm is originated from the π→π* transition for CC bond [22]. The band at 280 nm is from the π→π* transition for CN bond [30]. The absorption band at 350 nm is corresponding to the n→π* transitions of the CO /CN bonds [22,30]. Most of these absorption bands of CDs-1 in solid are detected in the fluorescence excitation spectrum (see the red curve in Fig. S2). The luminescence properties of CDs-0.5, CD-1, CDs-2 in the solid state and aqueous solution were studied in detail by fluorescence spectra. From the Fig. 3 a-c and Fig. S3, all the samples including CDs-0.5, CD-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs-4 in solid state exhibited relative broad emission with a typical excitation dependent behavior, which can be attributed to the existence of multiple emission sources. Meanwhile it is found that as the amount of Aphen increases, the blue light emitting part gradually weakens and the orange-yellow light part gradually increases, eventually leading to the red-shift of emission peak of the solid CDs gradually from the original double peaks to a single peak. Comparing with the solid-state fluorescence spectra of the above five kinds of CDs, the red-shift of the emission peak is mainly related to the increasing of the relative content of Aphen [22]. According to the related literature [6], the introduction of PVA will boost the blue-emitting part of the prepared CDs. When the Aphen is used as the reaction raw material, due to the formation of unique large conjugate system and strong rigidity, the low-energy state appears, so along with the content of Aphen increasing, the solid CDs showed a gradual weakening of the blue-light portion, and the red-light portion still existed. When there is no PVA in the reactants, the obtained CDs-3 hardly emitted light [6,22]. Since the PL emission of solid-state CDs-1 exhibits a certain excitation dependence, the emission peak gradually red-shifts as the excitation wavelength increases. Wonderfully, the CIE coordinates of CDs-1 gradually shift from (0.27, 0.32) to (0.38, 0.46) as the excitation wavelength is from 320 nm to 400 nm (Fig. S4). When the excitation wavelength is 365 nm, the white-light emission can be obtained for solid CDs-1 with CIE coordinate (0.32, 0.33). The solid-state fluorescence lifetime (LT) of the CDs-1 shows double exponential decay (τ1 = 0.61 ns, 76.0%, τ2 = 6.52 ns, 24.0%) with an average lifetime of 2.0 ns (Fig. S5), and the PL QYs is 7.0%. The above double exponential decay once again proves that there are two sources of fluorescence emission in the solid state of the CDs-1, which proves the above conjecture. Fig. 3d shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs-1 aqueous solution excited at 300–460 nm. It can be seen that the CDs-1 displays a certain excitation-dependent behavior in aqueous solution with a maximum emission at 420 nm excited at 320 nm. Furthermore, since the vibration dissipation of the CDs-1 in aqueous solution was not suppressed, no significant RTP was observed. From the PL decay curve (Fig. S6) of the CDs-1 aqueous solution, the LT of CDs-1 is a biexponential decay with an average lifetime of 3.98 ns. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for CDs-1 aqueous solution was determined to be 26%. Surprisingly, significant RTP was observed in the solid state of the CDs, and its phenomena and mechanism need to be further discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phosphorescence performance and mechanism of CDs-1
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that at room temperature, some types of CDs have obvious RTP (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S7a-e). Among them, CDs-1 has the strongest RTP intensity, and its phosphorescence spectra have a relatively wide range from 350 to 650 nm, mainly concentrated at 518 nm. The RTP lifetime decay curve of CDs-1 at 280 nm excitation can be fitted to a biexponential function (Fig. 4b) with an average phosphorescence LT of 103 ms (τ1 = 15.1 ms, 38.1%, τ2 = 157 ms, 61.9%) and the RTP is visible to the naked eye for around 3 s (Movie S1), which is longer than the fluorescence LT of CDs-1 in both solution and solid. The phosphorescence QY of CDs-1 is 5.3%. As can be seen from Fig. 4c, when the excitation wavelength is 260–400 nm, CDs-1 shows yellow-green RTP emission at 518 nm, indicating that the phosphorescence of CDs-1 almost has no excitation dependence. It is guessed that the phosphorescent source under different excitations comes from the same part of luminogens. Meanwhile the CDs-2 has relatively weak RTP, but the CDs-3 has almost no phosphorescence (Fig. 4a and Fig. S7c-d). As the ratio of Aphen to PVA in the reactants increases, the phosphorescence of the CDs shows a tendency to increase and then decrease, and CDs-1 has the strongest phosphorescence. At the same time, the emission peak of phosphorescence also shows a red-shift with the red-shift of the two fluorescence emission peaks of the solid CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 3a-c that as the amount of Aphen increases, the blue emission peak of the solid CDs gradually disappears, which is almost the same as the change of the intensity of phosphorescence. Thus, we suspect that the phosphorescence of the solid CDs is mainly derived from the blue fluorescence emission part. At the same time, the difference between the phosphorescence emission peak position of the CDs-1 and CDs-4 proves that the phosphorescence source is not derived from PVA only, and should be the result of the interaction of PVA and Aphen moieties. When PVA is used alone as a raw material to prepare CDs-4, blue-green phosphorescence with an emission band around 500 nm can be detected, and the phosphorescence intensity is greatly reduced than that of CDs-1 (Fig. S4e). The RTP phenomenon of CDs can be well explained by the following mechanism according to the related mechanism diagram such as Fig. 4d. It is well known that the realization of phosphorescence requires two steps: (1) ISC, this process can be assisted by the nitrogen in Aphen moieties and the CO/CN bonds of CDs, which proves the importance of Aphen for the RTP generation in solid CDs. (2) Suppression of non-radiative transition, the PVA chains, which are not fully carbonized, may form hydrogen bonds by itself or with Aphen moieties to increase the rigidity of the entire material, thereby helping to suppress non-radiative transitions. Only when both of the above conditions are met simultaneously, the RTP can be achieved [32–35]. To further verify the significant effect of PVA chains on the surface of CDs-1 that are not fully carbonized on RTP performance, we dissolved CDs-1 (0.05 g) and NH4F (0.1 g) in distilled water (5 mL) and measured the phosphorescence spectrum after lyophilization. The results showed that after the addition of excess NH4F (0.1 g), the phosphorescence intensity was largely quenched as shown in Fig. S8. We suspect that the F− of large electronegativity can form hydrogen bonds with PVA on the surface of CDs-1 that is not completely carbonized, which competes with the hydrogen bond in the CDs-1, thereby destroying the hydrogen bond in the CDs-1. The PVA's self-matrix protection in CDs-1 is greatly reduced, and the vibrational dissipation of the CDs-1 is not inhibited, so that the phosphorescence intensity is greatly reduced.

In general, it is difficult for the CDs to have phosphorescent properties because the solid CDs are easily affected by moisture, and the water affects the rigidity of the CDs. At the same time, the triplet excited state of the CDs material is easily quenched by oxygen [45–47]. If the CDs themselves do not have a barrier to block oxygen, it is difficult to achieve RTP [32]. In the process of forming CDs, as shown in Scheme 1 , Aphen molecules are first dissolved in water with the help of H3PO4 and co-carbonized with PVA under high temperature and high pressure [22]. Then, as the reaction proceeds, the excessive PVA chains dissolved in water are not fully carbonized and coated on the surface of the CDs, which not only prevents the aggregation of the CDs in the solid state, but also helps the CDs to achieve RTP. Because the PVA chains, which are not fully carbonized, first form a hydrogen bonds by itself or with the N atoms in the Aphen moieties, reinforcing the rigid structure of the entire material. At the same time, the not fully carbonized PVA chains can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules in the air, preventing them further penetrating into the interior of the CDs-1, and destroying the rigid structure of the CDs-1 [32]. In addition, the PVA chains on the surface of the CDs-1 can prevent the entry of oxygen molecules, protecting the triplet excitons from being quenched, thereby achieving RTP [45]. In addition, we observed that CDs-0.5 also has an RTP effect (Fig. 4a and Fig. S7a), but its phosphorescence intensity in air is weaker than that of CDs-1. This may be due to the fact that the amount of Aphen added is less and the ISC process is not sufficiently promoted, resulting in poorer RTP performance for the as-obtained CDs-0.5. As for CDs-2, a very weak RTP was observed in Fig. S7c. It may be due to that the content of Aphen is too much, and the formed luminophor structure is not completely restricted by the not fully carbonized PVA chains, resulting in an ACQ effect, so that it is difficult to form a sufficient rigid structure to emit phosphorescence. When PVA is used as a reactant alone, the green RTP can also be observed in CDs-4 (Fig. S7e), however, the emission peak intensity is weaker than that of CDs-1, and a corresponding blue-shift occurs [6]. In order to further verify the importance of the hydrogen bonding, we also conducted a comparative experiment. The Aphen was dissolved in water with H3PO4 to prepare CDs-3 by hydrothermal method. It can be seen from Fig. S7d that CDs-3 have almost no RTP because there are no PVA chains on CDs-3 surface to form a large number of hydrogen bonds to inhibit non-radiative transitions. The above experimental phenomena have once again proved the importance of hydrogen bonding in the process of realizing RTP. Therefore, the ratio between the reactants plays a crucial role in achieving RTP [45]. At the same time, the formation of hydrogen bonds causes the association of molecules, which generates a strong absorption band of OH on CDs, moving toward the low frequency at 3420 cm−1 (Fig. S9). Compared with the absorption peak of CDs-1, the OH absorption peak of CDs-1 (80% RH) shows a significant blue shift with the reduction of hydrogen bonds, accompanied by the disappearance of RTP, which indirectly verifies the importance of hydrogen bonding in achieving RTP [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of CDs-1 solution to different explosives
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous solution of CDs-1 prepared in this work has good stability and strong fluorescence intensity, which provides a prerequisite for its use as a fluorescent probe. In order to make the prepared CDs-1 aqueous solution more sensitive to the detection of explosives, we optimized the detection conditions. We firstly optimized the concentration of CDs-1 in water, and tested the fluorescence properties of different concentrations’ CDs-1 aqueous solution. It can be seen from Fig. S10 that when the concentration of CDs-1 is 0.3 mg mL−1, the CDs-1 aqueous solution has the strongest luminescence intensity. In order to detect the stability of the fluorescent probe, we tested the luminescence of the CDs-1 after they were placed at different times. As can be seen from Fig. S11a, when the aqueous solution of the CDs-1 was left at room temperature for six months, the luminescence intensity was still not obviously changed, indicating that the fluorescent probe has good stability. Finally, we also explored the influence of ion strength on the CDs-1′ luminescence. It can be seen from Fig. S11b that the luminescence intensity of CDs-1 with optimum concentration 0.3 mg mL−1 remains stable with increasing concentration of NaCl even when the concentration of NaCl is 1.0 M, indicating that the CDs-1 is less affected by the ionic strength. Therefore, different explosives were detected using the CDs-1 solution with a concentration of 0.3 mg mL−1. Fig. 5 a and b show the change in luminescence intensity and position of the CDs-1 when the explosive (6.68 μM) is added into the CDs-1 aqueous solution. In order to ensure that the solution of CDs-1 can detect explosives with high selectivity and sensitivity, TNP, TNT, 1,3-DNB, 2,6-DNT, 2,4-DNT, NB, NM, 4-NP, 2-NT, and phenol were detected respectively in optimal detection conditions. As presented in Fig. 5a, the PL quenching efficiency of TNP is much higher than that of other explosives, and there is no significant change for the position of emission peak. Fig. 5b is a corresponding histogram of PL response to different explosives. It can be seen that when TNP is added to 6.68 μM, according to η = (I0−I)/I0×100%, where I0 and I respectively represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs-1 before and after addition of TNP, and the quenching efficiency is about 60%. It indicates that the CDs-1 have strong selectivity for explosives [47 48]. Fig. 5c shows the PL change of the CDs-1 in aqueous solutions after adding TNP with various concentrations under optimal conditions. As the concentration of TNP increases, the PL intensity of the CDs-1 gradually decreases, and the position of the maximum emission peak does not change significantly. Fig. 5d is a plot of PL intensity versus TNP concentration for CDs-1. It can be seen that there is a decent linear relationship for the plot. At the same time, based on DL = 3 s/k (DL: detection limit, s: standard deviation of CD buffer solution without TNP, k: slope of the curve), the detection limit is determined to be 153 nM [47]. We also performed TNP sensing experiments in buffer solution of CDs-1 at pH = 2.25, 4.51, 9.66 and 11.56. From Fig. S12a, c, e, g, we can see the relationship between fluorescence intensity and TNP concentration at different pH values (pH = 2.25, 4.51, 9.66 and 11.56). However, their linear relationship is poor relative to the detection of TNP by the CDs-1 aqueous solution itself (Fig. S12b, d, f, h). At the same time, we calculated the detection limits in the same way, which are 256, 825, 728, and 935 nM at pH = 2.25, 4.51, 9.66 and 11.56. Therefore, it is not suitable for CDs-1 to be used to study the response to TNP under acidic and alkaline conditions by comprehensively comparing the above linear relationship and detection limit. Meanwhile we compared the detecting results of CDs-1 for TNP in this work with previous reported literatures (Table S1). Although there are many methods to detect TNP at this stage, there are problems in that most of the fluorescent probe are toxic, the preparation process is complicated, the organic solvent used for the detection is not environmentally friendly, and the detection limit is not up to the requirement. In this work, we used non-toxic CDs-1 as a probe for detecting TNP in aqueous solution, the detection limit is lower, which is an effective method for detecting TNP [29,34,46].

Based on the above experimental phenomena, we infer the possible mechanism of TNP-induced PL quenching for CDs-1 solution. Fig. 7a shows the excitation spectrum of CDs-1 and UV–vis absorption spectra of different explosives. It can be seen that TNP has a wide absorption band between 200–500 nm. At the same time, there is a large overlap between the excitation spectrum of CDs-1 and the absorption spectrum of TNP, while there is no significant overlap for other explosives. From the above analysis, the quenching mechanism of TNP to CDs-1 solution should be mainly attributed to the internal filtration effect (IFE) [47,48]. In order to further prove this mechanism, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-1 aqueous solution before and after adding 20 μL TNP were tested under different excitation wavelengths of 340, 400 and 440 nm. As displayed in Fig. 7b, there is almost no fluorescence quenching before and after TNP addition when 400 and 440 nm were used as the excitation wavelengths for CDs-1 aqueous solution to detect TNP. This should be that the UV–vis absorption band of TNP hardly overlaps with the long excitation wavelengths (400 and 440 nm). However, for the 340 nm excitation, there is partial overlap between the excitation spectrum of CDs-1 and the absorption spectrum of TNP, so the PL intensity of CDs-1 considerably descended after adding TNP. The above result demonstrates that the IFE can explain the quenching of CDs-1 by TNP [37–39].

We also verified other possible mechanisms that TNP can quench the fluorescence of CDs-1 solution. Comparing Fig. 3d and Fig. 7a, it can be concluded that there is a small overlap between the emission spectrum of CDs-1 solution and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TNP. We speculate that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may occur during the fluorescence quenching process. To further testify the existence of this mechanism, we tested the time resolved PL decay curves of CDs-1 aqueous solution before and after addition of TNP. As shown in Fig. S13, there is no change of fluorescence LT for the CDs-1 solution before and after the addition of TNP. Therefore, almost no FRET occurs during the quenching of CDS-1 by TNP. It is well known that the quenching mechanism of TNP for CDs-1 may be derived from static quenching effect (SQE) or dynamic quenching effect (DQE) [47,48]. When the FRET process occurs, the combination between the fluorophore and the quencher will cause a significant change in fluorescence LT [47,48]. At the same time, we gave the relevant UV test results and analysis of CDs-1, TNP and CDs-1+TNP (Fig. S14).

Compared the three curves in Fig. S14, the UV–vis absorption peak of the CDs-1 curve after adding TNP was significantly different from the UV–vis absorption peak of CDs-1 and TNP at 320–350 nm, indicating that a kind of nonfluorescent complex have been formed between the ground state emitter and the quencher, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of CDs-1. The above result demonstrates that the quenching mechanism of CDs-1 for TNP is SQE [47,48]. Therefore, according to the above analysis, the FRET process has little effect on the PL quenching of TNP on CDs-1, while the quenching mechanism should be ascribed to IFE and SQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of CDs-1 aqueous solution to pH values
ce:section.ce:para:
We have surprisingly found that the aqueous solution of CDs-1 prepared in this work, in addition to the selectivity and high sensitivity for detecting TNP, also has a specific responsiveness to pH with a strong regularity. As shown in Fig. 6
a, we measured the corresponding fluorescence by adding a certain amount of CDs-1 to the BR buffer solution of different pH so that the final concentration is 0.3 mg mL−1. It can be seen that the luminescence of the CDs-1 solution is enhanced under acidic conditions, and the position of the emission peak is significantly red-shifted. When the solution is alkaline, the position of the fluorescence peak of CDs-1 in the BR solution hardly changes, but the fluorescence intensity increases correspondingly. Fig. 6b shows the relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH, which is well fitted using the Boltzmann equation [49]. At the same time, we also quantitatively measured the corresponding relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-1 solution and pH values. It can be seen from Fig. 6c that there is a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH (pH = 2.23–5.52, pKa1 = 3.83), which the linear equation is y=−8010x + 63,884 with R2 = 0.9978. When pH is 5.52–11.56, the fluorescence intensity changes with pH as shown in Fig. 6d. It can be seen from the figure that R2 = 0.9762, y = 696.42x + 15,073 with the pKa2 = 8.50. The calculation of pKa is based on the Henderson-Hasselbach equation:
p H = p
K a
+ n log
I max
− I
I −
I min
Where Imax is the maximum fluorescence intensity, Imin is the minimum fluorescence intensity, I is the fluorescence intensity at the corresponding pH, and n is an empirical constant [50,51]. From the above results, the aqueous solution of CDs-1 has a regular response to pH. In order to further prove the shift of the emission peak for CDs-1 solution under acidic and alkaline conditions, we also tested the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-1 solution at different pH values. As displayed from Fig. S15, the UV–vis spectra of the CDs-1 solution red-shifted in acidic environment. In general, for the same luminescent substance, the larger absorption value, the larger the emission value, which explains the above phenomenon well [52–54]. According to previous work, the sensitivity of CDs to different pH is usually determined by the relevant functional groups of the substance molecules [2,55]. For CDs-1, the Aphen moieties on CDs-1 should be a critical for response to pH, and the intramolecular pyridine group can respond to H+ and OH− in acid or base. In an acidic environment, Aphen moieties in CDs-1 form a salt, which converts the functional group into a donor-acceptor (D-A) structure, increasing the degree of conjugation of the molecule, and thus causes the red-shift of emission peak of CDs-1 and enhances the PL intensity under acidic. However, this special D-A structure can’t be formed under alkaline conditions, so there is no significant change in the position of emission peak. In summary, the CDs-1 have a strong and regular response to pH, which lays a solid foundation for pH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs-1′ RTP in banknote security
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the solid-state RTP performance of CDs-1, we also tried to apply it to the anti-counterfeiting of banknotes, because the long-life of excitation state is difficult to copy and rare [27,45,56,57]. In particular, the solid CDs-1 can still emit strong phosphorescence as excited by 405 nm (Fig. S7b, f and Fig. 8 a). Upon 405 nm irradiation, the solid CDs-1 exhibits green fluorescence. After turning off the excitation, the green RTP can last 3 s by the naked eye observation. This indicates that the solid CDs-1 can be successfully applied in information encryption. At the same time, the damage to the material is much lower due to the longer excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 8b, the anti-counterfeiting coin does not display visible fluorescence and phosphorescence under ambient condition, but shows green fluorescence at a longer excitation of 405 nm. When the excitation light source is turned off, it is clear and easy to see that the banknotes attached with CDs-1 emits a green RTP, which are marked with "1″. Meanwhile it can be observed by the naked eye for a few seconds of RTP. In view of the fact that phosphorescence of the CDs-1 requires a longer excitation wavelength, so it has little damage to the banknote itself, and thus has great potential in advanced information security.

In summary, CDs-1 with liquid, solid-state fluorescence and RTP were synthesized from PVA and Aphen through one-step hydrothermal process. The RTP of the CDs-1 is derived from the CN/CN in Aphen moieties and CO produced during the carbonization process with a small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states, which helps to realize the ISC. At the same time, the RTP of CDs-1 is not quenched by moisture and oxygen in the air, because the formed hydrogen bonds between the incompletely carbonized PVA chains on the CDs-1′ surface and the Aphen moieties or other PVA chains inhibit non-radiative transitions, meanwhile the rigidity of Aphen itself and the fixation of CDs-1 also assist in the generation of RTP. The formation of hydrogen bonds inside and outside the CDs-1 protects them from moisture and oxygen. The aqueous solution of CDs-1 could be utilized for the selective sensing of TNP by IFE and SQE mechanism. In addition, the aqueous solution of the CDs-1 has a good responsiveness to pH values. The position of the emission peak under acidic conditions would red-shift, and the luminescence intensity would gradually increase as the pH decreased. It is worth noting that the CDs-1 synthesized by the one-step method have RTP without introducing any matrix, heavy atoms or metal ions, which can be used for anti-counterfeiting of coins or encryption, and decryption of security information. According to the above research results, we use the polymer as a reaction raw material to directly react with small organic molecules to achieve the versatile CDs with dual properties of fluorescence and phosphorescence, and propose a reasonable design idea for the novel multifunctional CDs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Feifei Zhao is currently studying her MS degree in the College of Chemistry at Northeast Normal University. Her research interests concern on the design and synthesis of carbon dots, room temperature phosphorescent materials and their application in fluorescent sensing.
Tianyi Zhang completed his BE and MS degree from Northeast Normal University, China, in 2019. His current research area is the preparation and applications of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots.

Qian Liu is studying her MS degree in the College of Chemistry at Northeast Normal University. Her researches focus on the design and synthesis of carbon dots with different structures and their application in fluorescence detection.

Changli Lü received his Ph.D. in polymer chemistry and physics in 2003 at Jilin University (Jilin, PR China). He is presently a professor of the College of Chemistry at Northeast Normal University. His current researches are in the design, synthesis and applications of functional polymers and their nanocomposites as well as multifunctional organic–inorganic hybrid nanomaterials.
